[[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[ja:%E7%94%B1%E5%B2%90%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Shrine | name = Yuki Shrine | image = [[File:Yuki-jinja 20100605.jpg|250px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}} {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}} {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}} Kurama Honmachi 1073 | position = {{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yuki Shrine}} | deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | founded = {{ill|940|ja|940年}} ({{ill|Tenkei|ja|天慶}} 3) | other names = Yuki Myōjin | festival = {{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}} ({{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}}) | map = {{location map|Japan Kyoto Prefecture|label=Yuki Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|relief=1|width=210|caption=}} }} '''Yuki Shrine''' (由岐神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}. Also referred to as '''Yuki Myōjin''' (靫明神), it enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} as its main deities, collectively called "Yuki Daimyōjin." The shrine serves as the {{ill|chinjusha|ja|鎮守社}} of {{ill|Kurama-dera|ja|鞍馬寺}}. == History == Originally, the enshrined deities were worshipped within the imperial palace. Following a series of disasters, including a major earthquake in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}} and the {{ill|Tengyō Rebellion|ja|天慶の乱}}, Emperor Suzaku ordered the relocation of the shrine to {{ill|Mount Kurama|ja|鞍馬山}} in {{ill|940|ja|940年}} (Tenkei 3). It was established as the chinju shrine of Kurama-dera to protect the northern border of the capital. The {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}} originates from the villagers' torchlight procession to welcome the shrine's deities during this relocation. The name "Yuki Myōjin" derives from the practice of offering {{ill|quiver|ja|靫}} (yuki) to the shrine during times of national crisis, praying for peace and stability. According to {{ill|Tsurezuregusa|ja|徒然草}}, this tradition was similar to practices at {{ill|Gojō Tenjin Shrine|ja|五條天神社}}, which enshrines the same deities. The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the {{ill|Kurama Cable Railway|ja|鞍馬山鋼索鉄道}} pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:History of Kyoto]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Kurama]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

The name "Yuki Myōjin" derives from the practice of offering {{ill|quiver|ja|靫}} (yuki) to the shrine during times of national crisis, praying for peace and stability. The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the {{ill|Kurama-dera Cable|ja|鞍馬山鋼索鉄道}} pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:History of Kyoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Kurama]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[ja:%E7%94%B1%E5%B2%90%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Shrine | name = Yuki Shrine | image = [[File:Yuki-jinja 20100605.jpg|250px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}} {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}} {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}} Kurama Honmachi 1073 | position = {{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yuki Shrine}} | deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | founded = {{ill|940|ja|940年}} ({{ill|Tenkei|ja|天慶}} 3) | other names = Yuki Myōjin | festival = {{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}} ({{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}}) | map = {{location map|Japan Kyoto Prefecture|label=Yuki Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|relief=1|width=210|caption=}} }} '''Yuki Shrine''' (由岐神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}. Also referred to as '''Yuki Myōjin''' (靫明神), it enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} as its main deities, collectively called "Yuki Daimyōjin." The shrine serves as the {{ill|chinjusha|ja|鎮守社}} of {{ill|Kurama-dera|ja|鞍馬寺}}. == History == Originally, the enshrined deities were worshipped within the imperial palace. Following a series of disasters, including a major earthquake in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}} and the {{ill|Tengyō Rebellion|ja|天慶の乱}}, Emperor Suzaku ordered the relocation of the shrine to {{ill|Mount Kurama|ja|鞍馬山}} in {{ill|940|ja|940年}} (Tenkei 3). It was established as the chinju shrine of Kurama-dera to protect the northern border of the capital. The {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}} originates from the villagers' torchlight procession to welcome the shrine's deities during this relocation. The name "Yuki Myōjin" derives from the practice of offering {{ill|quiver|ja|靫}} (yuki) to the shrine during times of national crisis, praying for peace and stability. According to {{ill|Tsurezuregusa|ja|徒然草}}, this tradition was similar to practices at {{ill|Gojō Tenjin Shrine|ja|五條天神社}}, which enshrines the same deities. The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the {{ill|Kurama-dera Cable|ja|鞍馬山鋼索鉄道}} pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:History of Kyoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Kurama]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[ja:%E7%94%B1%E5%B2%90%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Shrine | name = Yuki Shrine | image = [[File:Yuki-jinja 20100605.jpg|250px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}} {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}} {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}} Kurama Honmachi 1073 | position = {{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yuki Shrine}} | deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | founded = {{ill|940|ja|940年}} ({{ill|Tenkei|ja|天慶}} 3) | other names = Yuki Myōjin | festival = {{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}} ({{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}}) | map = {{location map|Japan Kyoto Prefecture|label=Yuki Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|relief=1|width=210|caption=}} }} '''Yuki Shrine''' (由岐神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}. Also referred to as '''Yuki Myōjin''' (靫明神), it enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} as its main deities, collectively called "Yuki Daimyōjin." The shrine serves as the {{ill|chinjusha|ja|鎮守社}} of {{ill|Kurama-dera|ja|鞍馬寺}}. == History == Originally, the enshrined deities were worshipped within the imperial palace. Following a series of disasters, including a major earthquake in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}} and the {{ill|Tengyō Rebellion|ja|天慶の乱}}, Emperor Suzaku ordered the relocation of the shrine to {{ill|Mount Kurama|ja|鞍馬山}} in {{ill|940|ja|940年}} (Tenkei 3). It was established as the chinju shrine of Kurama-dera to protect the northern border of the capital. The {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}} originates from the villagers' torchlight procession to welcome the shrine's deities during this relocation. The name "Yuki Myōjin" derives from the practice of offering {{ill|quiver|ja|靫}} (yuki) to the shrine during times of national crisis, praying for peace and stability. According to {{ill|Tsurezuregusa|ja|徒然草}}, this tradition was similar to practices at {{ill|Gojō Tenjin Shrine|ja|五條天神社}}, which enshrines the same deities. The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the {{ill|Kurama-dera Cable|ja|鞍馬山鋼索鉄道}} pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:History of Kyoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Kurama]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:fix the O]]{{DEFAULTSORTYuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:fix the O]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the {{ill|Kurama-dera Cable|ja|鞍馬山鋼索鉄道}} pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Chinjusha]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:History of Kyoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Kurama]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] [[Category:fix the O]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:fix the O]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

{{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[ja:%E7%94%B1%E5%B2%90%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Shrine | name = Yuki Shrine | image = [[File:Yuki-jinja 20100605.jpg|250px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}} {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}} {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}} Kurama Honmachi 1073 | position = {{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yuki Shrine}} | deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | founded = {{ill|940|ja|940年}} ({{ill|Tenkei|ja|天慶}} 3) | other names = Yuki Myōjin | festival = {{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}} ({{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}}) | map = {{location map|Japan Kyoto Prefecture|label=Yuki Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|relief=1|width=210|caption=}} }} '''Yuki Shrine''' (由岐神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}. Also referred to as '''Yuki Myōjin''' (靫明神), it enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} as its main deities, collectively called "Yuki Daimyōjin." The shrine serves as the {{ill|chinjusha|ja|鎮守社}} of {{ill|Kurama-dera|ja|鞍馬寺}}. == History == Originally, the enshrined deities were worshipped within the imperial palace. Following a series of disasters, including a major earthquake in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}} and the {{ill|Tengyō Rebellion|ja|天慶の乱}}, Emperor Suzaku ordered the relocation of the shrine to {{ill|Mount Kurama|ja|鞍馬山}} in {{ill|940|ja|940年}} (Tenkei 3). It was established as the chinju shrine of Kurama-dera to protect the northern border of the capital. The {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}} originates from the villagers' torchlight procession to welcome the shrine's deities during this relocation. The name "Yuki Myōjin" derives from the practice of offering {{ill|quiver|ja|靫}} (yuki) to the shrine during times of national crisis, praying for peace and stability. According to {{ill|Tsurezuregusa|ja|徒然草}}, this tradition was similar to practices at {{ill|Gojō Tenjin Shrine|ja|五條天神社}}, which enshrines the same deities. The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the {{ill|Kurama-dera Cable|ja|鞍馬山鋼索鉄道}} pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Chinjusha]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:History of Kyoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Kurama]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{DEFAULTSORT::Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[ja:%E7%94%B1%E5%B2%90%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Shrine | name = Yuki Shrine | image = [[File:Yuki-jinja 20100605.jpg|250px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}} {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}} {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}} Kurama Honmachi 1073 | position = {{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yuki Shrine}} | deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | founded = {{ill|940|ja|940年}} ({{ill|Tenkei|ja|天慶}} 3) | other names = Yuki Myōjin | festival = {{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}} ({{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}}) | map = {{location map|Japan Kyoto Prefecture|label=Yuki Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|relief=1|width=210|caption=}} }} '''Yuki Shrine''' (由岐神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}. Also referred to as '''Yuki Myōjin''' (靫明神), it enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} as its main deities, collectively called "Yuki Daimyōjin." The shrine serves as the {{ill|chinjusha|ja|鎮守社}} of {{ill|Kurama-dera|ja|鞍馬寺}}. == History == Originally, the enshrined deities were worshipped within the imperial palace. Following a series of disasters, including a major earthquake in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}} and the {{ill|Tengyō Rebellion|ja|天慶の乱}}, Emperor Suzaku ordered the relocation of the shrine to {{ill|Mount Kurama|ja|鞍馬山}} in {{ill|940|ja|940年}} (Tenkei 3). It was established as the chinju shrine of Kurama-dera to protect the northern border of the capital. The {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}} originates from the villagers' torchlight procession to welcome the shrine's deities during this relocation. The name "Yuki Myōjin" derives from the practice of offering {{ill|quiver|ja|靫}} (yuki) to the shrine during times of national crisis, praying for peace and stability. According to {{ill|Tsurezuregusa|ja|徒然草}}, this tradition was similar to practices at {{ill|Gojō Tenjin Shrine|ja|五條天神社}}, which enshrines the same deities. The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the {{ill|Kurama-dera Cable|ja|鞍馬山鋼索鉄道}} pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Chinjusha]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:History of Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Kurama]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:defautsort]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the [[:en:Kurama-dera_Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]] pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Chinjusha]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:History of Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Kurama]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[ja:%E7%94%B1%E5%B2%90%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Shrine | name = Yuki Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuki-jinja 20100605.jpg|250px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}} {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}} {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}} Kurama Honmachi 1073 | position = {{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yuki Shrine}} | deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | founded = {{ill|940|ja|940年}} ({{ill|Tenkei|ja|天慶}} 3) | other names = Yuki Myōjin | festival = {{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}} ({{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}}) | map = {{location map|Japan Kyoto Prefecture|label=Yuki Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|relief=1|width=210|caption=}} }} '''Yuki Shrine''' (由岐神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}. Also referred to as '''Yuki Myōjin''' (靫明神), it enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} as its main deities, collectively called "Yuki Daimyōjin." The shrine serves as the {{ill|chinjusha|ja|鎮守社}} of {{ill|Kurama-dera|ja|鞍馬寺}}. == History == Originally, the enshrined deities were worshipped within the imperial palace. Following a series of disasters, including a major earthquake in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}} and the {{ill|Tengyō Rebellion|ja|天慶の乱}}, Emperor Suzaku ordered the relocation of the shrine to {{ill|Mount Kurama|ja|鞍馬山}} in {{ill|940|ja|940年}} (Tenkei 3). It was established as the chinju shrine of Kurama-dera to protect the northern border of the capital. The {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}} originates from the villagers' torchlight procession to welcome the shrine's deities during this relocation. The name "Yuki Myōjin" derives from the practice of offering {{ill|quiver|ja|靫}} (yuki) to the shrine during times of national crisis, praying for peace and stability. According to {{ill|Tsurezuregusa|ja|徒然草}}, this tradition was similar to practices at {{ill|Gojō Tenjin Shrine|ja|五條天神社}}, which enshrines the same deities. The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the [[:en:Kurama-dera_Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]] pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Chinjusha]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:History of Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Kurama]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[ja:%E7%94%B1%E5%B2%90%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Shrine | name = Yuki Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuki-jinja 20100605.jpg|250px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}} {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}} {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}} Kurama Honmachi 1073 | position = {{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yuki Shrine}} | deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | founded = {{ill|940|ja|940年}} ({{ill|Tenkei|ja|天慶}} 3) | other names = Yuki Myōjin | festival = {{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}} ({{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}}) | map = {{location map|Japan Kyoto Prefecture|label=Yuki Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|relief=1|width=210|caption=}} }} '''Yuki Shrine''' (由岐神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}. Also referred to as '''Yuki Myōjin''' (靫明神), it enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} as its main deities, collectively called "Yuki Daimyōjin." The shrine serves as the {{ill|chinjusha|ja|鎮守社}} of {{ill|Kurama-dera|ja|鞍馬寺}}. == History == Originally, the enshrined deities were worshipped within the imperial palace. Following a series of disasters, including a major earthquake in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}} and the {{ill|Tengyō Rebellion|ja|天慶の乱}}, Emperor Suzaku ordered the relocation of the shrine to {{ill|Mount Kurama|ja|鞍馬山}} in {{ill|940|ja|940年}} (Tenkei 3). It was established as the chinju shrine of Kurama-dera to protect the northern border of the capital. The {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}} originates from the villagers' torchlight procession to welcome the shrine's deities during this relocation. The name "Yuki Myōjin" derives from the practice of offering {{ill|quiver|ja|靫}} (yuki) to the shrine during times of national crisis, praying for peace and stability. According to {{ill|Tsurezuregusa|ja|徒然草}}, this tradition was similar to practices at {{ill|Gojō Tenjin Shrine|ja|五條天神社}}, which enshrines the same deities. The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the [[:en:Kurama-dera_Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]] pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Chinjusha]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:History of Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Kurama]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]]{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the [[:en:Kurama-dera_Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]] pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Chinjusha]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:History of Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Kurama]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)-qq]] [[Category:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]]{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}} {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}} {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}} Kurama Honmachi 1073 | position = {{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yuki Shrine}} | deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | founded = {{ill|940|ja|940年}} ({{ill|Tenkei|ja|天慶}} 3) | other names = Yuki Myōjin | festival = {{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}} ({{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}}) | map = {{location map|Japan Kyoto Prefecture|label=Yuki Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|relief=1|width=210|caption=}} }} '''Yuki Shrine''' (由岐神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}. Also referred to as '''Yuki Myōjin''' (靫明神), it enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} as its main deities, collectively called "Yuki Daimyōjin." The shrine serves as the {{ill|chinjusha|ja|鎮守社}} of {{ill|Kurama-dera|ja|鞍馬寺}}. == History == Originally, the enshrined deities were worshipped within the imperial palace. Following a series of disasters, including a major earthquake in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}} and the {{ill|Tengyō Rebellion|ja|天慶の乱}}, Emperor Suzaku ordered the relocation of the shrine to {{ill|Mount Kurama|ja|鞍馬山}} in {{ill|940|ja|940年}} (Tenkei 3). It was established as the chinju shrine of Kurama-dera to protect the northern border of the capital. The {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}} originates from the villagers' torchlight procession to welcome the shrine's deities during this relocation. The name "Yuki Myōjin" derives from the practice of offering {{ill|quiver|ja|靫}} (yuki) to the shrine during times of national crisis, praying for peace and stability. According to {{ill|Tsurezuregusa|ja|徒然草}}, this tradition was similar to practices at {{ill|Gojō Tenjin Shrine|ja|五條天神社}}, which enshrines the same deities. Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the [[:en:Kurama-dera_Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]] pass by Yuki Shrine. {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[ja:%E7%94%B1%E5%B2%90%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuki Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuki-jinja 20100605.jpg|250px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}} {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}} {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}} Kurama Honmachi 1073 | position = {{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yuki Shrine}} | deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | founded = {{ill|940|ja|940年}} ({{ill|Tenkei|ja|天慶}} 3) | other names = Yuki Myōjin | festival = {{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}} ({{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}}) | map = {{location map|Japan Kyoto Prefecture|label=Yuki Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|relief=1|width=210|caption=}} }} '''Yuki Shrine''' (由岐神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}. Also referred to as '''Yuki Myōjin''' (靫明神), it enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} as its main deities, collectively called "Yuki Daimyōjin." The shrine serves as the {{ill|chinjusha|ja|鎮守社}} of {{ill|Kurama-dera|ja|鞍馬寺}}. == History == Originally, the enshrined deities were worshipped within the imperial palace. Following a series of disasters, including a major earthquake in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}} and the {{ill|Tengyō Rebellion|ja|天慶の乱}}, Emperor Suzaku ordered the relocation of the shrine to {{ill|Mount Kurama|ja|鞍馬山}} in {{ill|940|ja|940年}} (Tenkei 3). It was established as the chinju shrine of Kurama-dera to protect the northern border of the capital. The {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}} originates from the villagers' torchlight procession to welcome the shrine's deities during this relocation. The name "Yuki Myōjin" derives from the practice of offering {{ill|quiver|ja|靫}} (yuki) to the shrine during times of national crisis, praying for peace and stability. According to {{ill|Tsurezuregusa|ja|徒然草}}, this tradition was similar to practices at {{ill|Gojō Tenjin Shrine|ja|五條天神社}}, which enshrines the same deities. The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the [[:en:Kurama-dera_Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]] pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Chinjusha]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:History of Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Kurama]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)-qq]] [[Category:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]]{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the [[:en:Kurama-dera_Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]] pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Chinjusha]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:History of Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Kurama]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)-qq]] [[Category:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]]{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[ja:%E7%94%B1%E5%B2%90%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuki Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuki-jinja 20100605.jpg|250px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}} {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}} {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}} Kurama Honmachi 1073 | position = {{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yuki Shrine}} | deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | founded = {{ill|940|ja|940年}} ({{ill|Tenkei|ja|天慶}} 3) | other names = Yuki Myōjin | festival = {{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}} ({{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}}) | map = {{location map|Japan Kyoto Prefecture|label=Yuki Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|relief=1|width=210|caption=}} }} '''Yuki Shrine''' (由岐神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}. Also referred to as '''Yuki Myōjin''' (靫明神), it enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} as its main deities, collectively called "Yuki Daimyōjin." The shrine serves as the {{ill|chinjusha|ja|鎮守社}} of {{ill|Kurama-dera|ja|鞍馬寺}}. == History == Originally, the enshrined deities were worshipped within the imperial palace. Following a series of disasters, including a major earthquake in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}} and the {{ill|Tengyō Rebellion|ja|天慶の乱}}, Emperor Suzaku ordered the relocation of the shrine to {{ill|Mount Kurama|ja|鞍馬山}} in {{ill|940|ja|940年}} (Tenkei 3). It was established as the chinju shrine of Kurama-dera to protect the northern border of the capital. The {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}} originates from the villagers' torchlight procession to welcome the shrine's deities during this relocation. The name "Yuki Myōjin" derives from the practice of offering {{ill|quiver|ja|靫}} (yuki) to the shrine during times of national crisis, praying for peace and stability. According to {{ill|Tsurezuregusa|ja|徒然草}}, this tradition was similar to practices at {{ill|Gojō Tenjin Shrine|ja|五條天神社}}, which enshrines the same deities. The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the [[:en:Kurama-dera_Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]] pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto City]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Chinjusha]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Momoyama-period architecture]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Buildings in Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:History of Kyoto]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:Kurama]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}} [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)-qq]] [[Category:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]]{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:由岐神社]]

{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}{{citation needed|Yuki Shrine (Kyoto)}}[[ja:%E7%94%B1%E5%B2%90%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuki Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuki-jinja 20100605.jpg|250px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E}}}} | location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府}} {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}} {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}} Kurama Honmachi 1073 | position = {{coord|35|06|57.9|N|135|46|17.1|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yuki Shrine}} | deities = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} | founded = {{ill|940|ja|940年}} ({{ill|Tenkei|ja|天慶}} 3) | other names = Yuki Myōjin | festival = {{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}} ({{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}}) | map = {{location map|Japan Kyoto Prefecture|label=Yuki Shrine|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|relief=1|width=210|caption=}} }} '''Yuki Shrine''' (由岐神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Sakyō-ku|ja|左京区}}, {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都市}}. Also referred to as '''Yuki Myōjin''' (靫明神), it enshrines {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}} and {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} as its main deities, collectively called "Yuki Daimyōjin." The shrine serves as the {{ill|chinjusha|ja|鎮守社}} of {{ill|Kurama-dera|ja|鞍馬寺}}. == History == Originally, the enshrined deities were worshipped within the imperial palace. Following a series of disasters, including a major earthquake in {{ill|Heian-kyō|ja|平安京}} and the {{ill|Tengyō Rebellion|ja|天慶の乱}}, Emperor Suzaku ordered the relocation of the shrine to {{ill|Mount Kurama|ja|鞍馬山}} in {{ill|940|ja|940年}} (Tenkei 3). It was established as the chinju shrine of Kurama-dera to protect the northern border of the capital. The {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬の火祭}} originates from the villagers' torchlight procession to welcome the shrine's deities during this relocation. The name "Yuki Myōjin" derives from the practice of offering {{ill|quiver|ja|靫}} (yuki) to the shrine during times of national crisis, praying for peace and stability. According to {{ill|Tsurezuregusa|ja|徒然草}}, this tradition was similar to practices at {{ill|Gojō Tenjin Shrine|ja|五條天神社}}, which enshrines the same deities. The worship hall and main hall were rebuilt in {{ill|1607|ja|1607年}} (Keichō 12) under the patronage of Toyotomi Hideyori. == Enshrined Deities == * Main deities: {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|ja|少彦名命}} * Auxiliary deities: Eight additional kami == Grounds == * Main Hall - Renovated in 2023. * Sanpōkōjin Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kōjin|ja|三宝荒神}}, revered as a deity of fire and hearth protection. * Shrine Office * Shironaga Benzaiten Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}. * Kanja Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚命}}, a deity of prosperity. * Iwakami Shrine - Dedicated to {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主命}} and {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|ja|大山祇命}}. * Ōsugi Shrine - Features sacred trees, including three cedar trees and one {{ill|Machilus|ja|カゴノキ}}. * {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}} * Worship Hall (割拝殿) - Designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|ja|重要文化財}} in the Momoyama architectural style, rebuilt in Keichō 12 (1607). == Cultural Properties == === Important Cultural Properties === * Worship Hall (割拝殿) * Pair of stone komainu - Deposited at the {{ill|Kyoto National Museum|ja|京都国立博物館}}, displayed as "stone lions"{{Cite web|url=http://www.yukijinjya.jp/about/index.html|title=About Yuki Shrine|publisher=Yuki Shrine|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.kyohaku.go.jp/jp/theme/floor1_5/f1_5_koremade/shishi_2016.html|title=Special Exhibit: Lions and Komainu|publisher=Kyoto National Museum|accessdate=October 6, 2019}}. === Kyoto City Natural Monuments === * Sacred trees of Ōsugi Shrine (three cedar trees and one machilus) === Kyoto City Intangible Folk Cultural Properties === * {{ill|Kurama Fire Festival|ja|鞍馬火祭}} == Additional Information == * Entry to the shrine grounds is free. However, since it is within the precincts of Kurama-dera, visitors must pay the temple's admission fee of 500 yen for high school students and older (free for younger children and discounts for Eizan Railway one-day pass holders). Early morning and evening access is free. * Visitors using the [[:en:Kurama-dera_Cable|Kurama-dera Cable]] pass by Yuki Shrine. To visit after worshiping at the Kurama-dera Main Hall, visitors must walk back down the approach. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|ja|豊臣秀頼}} * {{ill|Momoyama Period|ja|安土桃山時代}} * {{ill|Shinto Architecture|ja|神社建築}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yuki-jinja}} * [http://www.yukijinjya.jp Official Website of Yuki Shrine] {{Shinto Sidebar}}
